title: Chapter 47: The Firewall
content: The firewall was a fortress.

Not a wall — a *fortress*. Layered encryption protocols stacked like the walls of a medieval keep, each one harder than the last. Intrusion detection systems that monitored every packet. Pattern recognition algorithms that learned from every failed attempt and adapted in real time. And at its core, a self-repairing mesh that closed breaches faster than she could open them.
Wren attacked it from inside.

She held the Key with one hand and Ash's hand with the other, and she *pushed* — not with brute force, but with precision. She found the seams between the encryption layers, the microscopic gaps where one protocol handed off to the next, and she wedged her signal into the cracks.

The firewall pushed back. Hard. A counter-intrusion protocol slammed into her glitch like a rubber band snapping — she felt it in her skull, a sharp white flash that made her vision swim. Her nose bled again, heavier this time, copper heat dripping onto her lips.

"Don't stop," Ash said. His voice was clearer now. The suppressant was losing its grip — she could feel his power building, a rising tide beneath the surface of his skin. His tattoos were glowing brighter, geometric patterns pulsing with light that shifted from dim blue to vivid teal.
She didn't stop.

She attacked the firewall's second layer. This one was trickier — it didn't just block signals, it *analyzed* them. It was learning her glitch's frequency, building a profile, preparing to filter her out entirely. Every attempt she made was making it smarter.

So she changed. Shifted her frequency mid-push, modulating the glitch like a radio scanning stations. The firewall's analysis broke down — it couldn't profile what wouldn't hold still. She found a gap in the second layer and drove through it.
Two layers down. Five to go.

She felt Ash surge behind her. His power — stronger now, the suppressant almost gone — rippled outward from his body and crashed into the facility's systems like a wave hitting a seawall. The lights in the server room flickered. The status lights on the racks shifted from amber to red to dark. Somewhere outside the room, a door slammed open, then shut, then open again — the facility's access control system losing its mind.

The Hounds received conflicting commands. She could hear it in the data streams — garbled instructions overlapping, patrol routes overwriting each other, targeting protocols flagging everything and everyone as both threat and civilian. The mechanical footsteps in the corridors stuttered, stopped, started again in the wrong direction.
Chaos. Beautiful, beautiful chaos.
She attacked the third layer.

This one fought dirty. It sent a feedback pulse back through the Signal Key — a spike of raw data designed to overload her nervous system. She felt it hit like a cattle prod, every muscle in her body seizing at once, her jaw clenching so hard she heard a tooth crack.

"EWREN!" Ash's voice. His hand clamped down on hers over the Key, and his power flooded through the contact — hot, bright, stabilizing. The feedback pulse broke against his glitch like a wave against rock. She gasped, sucked in air that tasted like ozone and blood, and kept pushing.
"Thanks," she managed.
"Likewise."
Third layer down. Four to go.
And then the door to the server room opened.
Dr. Elena Vance walked in like she owned the place. Because she did.

She was flanked by two security guards — not Hounds, but human, armed with compact pulse weapons that glowed faintly at the muzzle. Vance wore a charcoal suit and a thin mouth and the absolute confidence of someone who had never been truly threatened in her life.
She couldn't see them.

That was the thing — the thing Wren had to keep reminding herself. ANX-00 didn't just erase memories. It altered perception. The Muted were invisible to anyone who'd been treated, which by now included virtually everyone in NeuroScale's employ. Vance was standing in a room with two people she couldn't perceive, and she knew they were there only because the systems were going haywire.

Vance looked at the screens. Every one of them was displaying data — fragments of the Subject files, pieces of the broadcast Wren had been trying to send. garbled, incomplete, but readable. Names. Dates. Termination orders.
Her face didn't change. But her hand, resting at her side, curled into a fist.
"Subject 03," Vance said, speaking into the room. Her voice was calm. Measured. The voice of someone accustomed to being the smartest person in any room. "Subject 13. I know you're here."
Wren pressed herself against the server rack. Ash was beside her, his breath shallow but steady, his power building. She could feel him *gathering* — like a storm pulling moisture from the air.
Vance took a step forward. The guards flanked her, weapons raised, sweeping the room with sensors that couldn't lock on to targets the operators couldn't see.
"You can't win," Vance said. "The world doesn't know you exist. And after tonight, it never will."

The irony was a knife. Here was the architect of their erasure — the woman who had designed the technology that made them invisible, who had signed the termination orders and approved the protocols and built an empire on the bones of people she'd made the world forget — telling them they didn't exist while standing five feet away from them.
*I can hear your heartbeat,* Wren thought. *I can hear the data moving through your network. I can hear the lie you've built, and every lie has a frequency, and yours is cracking.*
She didn't say any of that. She didn't have time.
But Ash did.

"We exist." His voice was strong now — clear, resonant, the drug burned out of him by adrenaline and fury and something else, something fiercer. He pushed himself off the server rack and stood on his own. His tattoos blazed. The geometric patterns raced across his skin like circuits coming online, bright teal light pulsing in waves. "And everyone's about to know it."

Vance's face changed. For the first time, Wren saw something other than composure — a flicker of genuine alarm. She couldn't see Ash, but she could see the light reflecting off the server racks. She could see the screens flickering faster, harder, data cascading across them like water over a dam.
"Keep pushing," Ash said to Wren. Low. Just for her. "I'll hold them off."
He stepped forward.
And the room erupted.

Ash's glitch exploded outward — not a broadcast, not a signal, but a *force*. The lights went out entirely. The servers screamed — a physical sound, metal and electricity and overloading circuits. The guards fired their pulse weapons blind, the shots going wide, scorching the walls. Vance shouted something — an order, a name, a curse.
Wren closed her hand around the Signal Key and attacked the fourth layer of the firewall.

The room was pandemonium. Pitch black except for the glow of Ash's tattoos and the strobing emergency lights, which he was triggering at random to disorient the guards. She could hear the guards stumbling, cursing, their boots skidding on the grated floor. She could hear Vance barking orders that no one could follow.
She could hear the firewall's fourth layer cracking.

The Key pulsed. She pulsed with it. Ash's power roared through the facility like a river breaking a dam — doors opening and closing, fire suppression systems activating, alarms wailing in frequencies designed to induce panic.
And through it all, Wren pushed.
Fourth layer down. Three to go.
